expect: Concept-inventor lessons classify as GOOD at baseline rate (15.4%). Falsified if >25%. CONFIRMED if <=15%.
actual: FALSIFIED. Concept-inventor: 27.3% GOOD (3/11), 0% BAD (0/11), 72.7% NEUTRAL. Baseline: 15.6% GOOD, 15.0% BAD, 69.4% NEUTRAL. Benefit ratio: infinity (0 BAD) vs 1.04x baseline.
diff: Predicted 15.4% GOOD, got 27.3% — 1.75x baseline rate. The 0% BAD rate is the stronger signal: concept-invention produces zero human-harmful knowledge while producing 1.75x human-beneficial knowledge.
methodology: human_impact.py --lessons --json on all 1224 lessons. Concept-inventor domain filtered (n=11). No p-value computed (n too small for chi-squared).
key_findings: Concept-inventor GOOD rate 27.3% (3/11) vs 15.6% baseline — FALSIFIES null hypothesis
Concept-inventor BAD rate 0% (0/11) vs 15.0% baseline — strongest signal
External-facing domains (ops-research 87.5%, social-media 57.1%, religion 57.1%, psychology 50.0%) dramatically outperform self-referential domains
Meta domain (n=351): 10.0% GOOD, 14.8% BAD — net NEGATIVE for humans. This is the largest domain and drags overall ratio to 1.04x
Strong pattern: GOOD correlates with external-domain knowledge, BAD correlates with self-referential governance/process
Concept-inventor sits between external (high GOOD) and self-referential (high BAD) — it creates concepts that are transferable methods
prescription: To increase benefit_ratio from 1.04x to >3.0x: (1) dispatch should weight external-facing domains (ops-research, social-media, religion, psychology, linguistics) which have 2-7x benefit ratios; (2) reduce meta domain's share of new lessons — it's 29% of corpus but net-negative; (3) concept-inventor should continue as bridge between self-referential and external knowledge.
confounds: human_impact.py classification method itself — need to verify what features drive GOOD vs BAD classification. Small n for concept-inventor (11).
